tier_1_regimens.notes: Concurrent platinum-containing chemotherapy with EBRT utilizes cisplatin as a single agent. Pembrolizumab may be added with CRT as follows: cisplatin/RT and pembrolizumab (FIGO 2014 stage IIIA, IIIB, and IVA: category 1); cisplatin/RT and pembrolizumab (select FIGO 2018 stage III–IVA: category 2B). Category 2B is for FIGO 2018 stage IIIC purely on nodal metastasis without concomitant tumor characteristics defined in 2014 FIGO stage IIIA–IIIB. If the FIGO 2018 staging of stage III overlaps with FIGO 2014 IIIA/IIIB staging, category 1 can be applied. [1]

tier_2_handbook.definition: Cervical cancer refers to malignant neoplasms arising from the uterine cervix, a fibromuscular organ located at the lower end of the uterus, connecting the uterine cavity to the vaginal canal. The cervix is divided into two main regions: the ectocervix (visible portion protruding into the vagina, lined by non-keratinizing squamous epithelium) and the endocervix (inner canal, lined by mucus-secreting glandular epithelium). The transformation zone, the junction between these two epithelial types, is the critical anatomical site where most cervical cancers originate, as this region undergoes continuous metaplastic changes and is particularly susceptible to human papillomavirus (HPV) infection and neoplastic transformation [1-4]. Cervical cancer is a histologically heterogeneous disease. The NCCN Guidelines for Cervical Cancer include the management of squamous cell carcinoma, adenosquamous carcinoma, adenocarcinoma of the cervix, and small cell neuroendocrine carcinoma of the cervix [MS-3]. Squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) is the most common histologic type, accounting for approximately 80% to 90% of all cervical carcinomas worldwide [MS-2]. Adenocarcinoma accounts for approximately 20% of all cervical cancers, and adenocarcinoma and adenosquamous carcinoma (ASC) together make up the remaining proportion [MS-2]. ASC is rare, accounting for approximately 5% to 6% of all cervical carcinomas [MS-17]. Small cell neuroendocrine carcinoma (NECC) accounts for approximately 1% to 1.5% of all cervical cancers and, while rare, the cervix is the most common site for neuroendocrine carcinoma (including small cell and large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma) in the female genital tract [MS-40]. Glassy-cell carcinomas, sarcomas, and other histologic types are not within the scope of the NCCN Guidelines [MS-3]. The earliest stages of cervical carcinoma may be asymptomatic or associated with watery vaginal discharge, postcoital bleeding, or intermittent spotting [MS-3]. Because of the accessibility of the uterine cervix, cervical cytology (Papanicolaou or Pap tests) and cervical biopsies can usually result in an accurate diagnosis [MS-3]. The revised 2018 FIGO staging system stratifies cervical cancer from stage I (confined to the cervix) through stage IV (extending beyond the true pelvis or involving the mucosa of the bladder or rectum), with further subdivisions based on depth of invasion, tumor size, and nodal involvement [ST-1].

subtypes.name: Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) — HPV-Associated
subtypes.description: Cervical squamous cell carcinoma is a squamous epithelial tumor with stromal invasion and/or exophytic invasion. It accounts for approximately 80% to 90% of cervical carcinomas worldwide. The majority of cervical SCCs (>90%) are HPV-associated, with high-risk HPV 16 and 18 being the most common types. Histologic patterns include keratinizing (presence of keratin pearls), non-keratinizing, basaloid (nests of basal-type squamous cells), warty (condylomatous), and papillary types. Non-keratinizing and basaloid patterns are most commonly noted with HPV-associated tumors. Almost all HPV-associated SCCs show strong and diffuse p16 overexpression in nuclei and cytoplasm by immunohistochemistry (IHC).
subtypes.frequency: 80%–90% of all cervical carcinomas; >90% of SCCs are HPV-associated [CERV-A 2 of 7, MS-2]
subtypes.key_associations: High-risk HPV types 16 and 18; strong and diffuse p16 overexpression by IHC; generally favorable prognosis compared to HPV-independent SCC [CERV-A 2 of 7]

subtypes.name: Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) — HPV-Independent
subtypes.description: HPV-independent cervical SCC is a relatively recently described entity, with 5%–7% of cervical SCCs reported to be HPV-negative. These tumors typically present later in life (7th decade) and at an advanced stage. HPV-independent cervical SCCs have been described to demonstrate TP53, KRAS, ARID1A, and PTEN mutations. Morphologically, HPV-independent SCCs are usually of the keratinizing type. Morphology alone is unreliable for determining HPV association, and utility of p16 IHC and/or molecular HPV typing is recommended for establishing HPV status [CERV-A 2 of 7].
subtypes.key_associations: TP53, KRAS, ARID1A, and PTEN mutations; keratinizing morphology; older age at presentation; advanced stage at diagnosis; less favorable prognosis [CERV-A 2 of 7, MS-2]

subtypes.name: Endocervical Adenocarcinoma — HPV-Associated (Usual Type)
subtypes.description: Endocervical adenocarcinoma is a gland-forming tumor typically originating in the transformation zone, demonstrating stromal invasion and/or expansile type invasion. Adenocarcinomas of the cervix can be HPV-associated (high-risk HPV types 18, 16, and 45 most commonly) or HPV-independent. The usual type accounts for approximately 75% of HPV-associated cases and includes villoglandular and micropapillary subtypes. Mucinous types include intestinal, signet-ring cell, and invasive stratified mucin-producing carcinoma (ISMC). 95% of HPV-associated carcinomas will demonstrate block-type p16 expression by IHC. The Silva classification system is used for evaluating patterns of invasion in HPV-associated endocervical adenocarcinoma, subdividing these tumors into three categories (A, B, C) with Pattern A tumors having excellent survival and no lymph node metastases or recurrences [CERV-A 3 of 7, CERV-A 4 of 7, MS-10, MS-11].
subtypes.frequency: Approximately 75% of HPV-associated adenocarcinomas; adenocarcinomas as a group account for ~20% of all cervical cancers [CERV-A 4 of 7, MS-2]
subtypes.key_associations: High-risk HPV types 18, 16, and 45; block-type p16 expression; Silva Pattern A (excellent prognosis); better clinical outcomes compared to HPV-independent adenocarcinomas [CERV-A 4 of 7]

subtypes.name: Endocervical Adenocarcinoma — HPV-Independent (Gastric Type)
subtypes.description: Gastric-type adenocarcinoma makes up approximately 10%–15% of cervical adenocarcinomas (with up to 25% noted in Japan). It is found in association with Peutz-Jeghers syndrome (STK11 mutation), and while typically negative for p16 block expression, it may show aberrant p53 expression in approximately 50% of cases. These tumors show a high prevalence of invasion, extrauterine spread, and present at an advanced stage. They are considered inappropriate for fertility-sparing surgery due to their diagnostic challenges and potentially aggressive nature [CERV-A 4 of 7, MS-17].
subtypes.frequency: 10%–15% of cervical adenocarcinomas (up to 25% in Japan) [CERV-A 4 of 7]
subtypes.key_associations: Peutz-Jeghers syndrome (STK11 mutation); aberrant p53 expression (~50%); typically negative for p16 block expression; advanced stage at presentation; high invasion and extrauterine spread [CERV-A 4 of 7, MS-17]

subtypes.name: Endocervical Adenocarcinoma — HPV-Independent (Clear Cell)
subtypes.description: Clear cell carcinomas make up 3% of endocervical adenocarcinomas, occurring sporadically or in association with in-utero diethylstilbestrol (DES) exposure. These tumors may demonstrate diffuse p16 expression despite the absence of HPV infection. In these instances, high-risk HPV in situ hybridization (ISH) can be utilized to confirm HPV status [CERV-A 4 of 7, MS-17].
subtypes.key_associations: In-utero DES exposure; may show diffuse p16 despite HPV-negative status; requires HPV ISH for accurate classification [CERV-A 4 of 7]

subtypes.name: Endocervical Adenocarcinoma — HPV-Independent (Mesonephric)
subtypes.description: Mesonephric type demonstrates mesonephric (Wolffian) differentiation and is associated with mesonephric remnants. It typically is located deep in the lateral wall and histologically demonstrates architectural crowding, haphazard infiltration, atypia, mitotic activity, and necrosis. GATA-3 and CD10 IHC stains will be positive in the tumor and associated mesonephric remnants [CERV-A 4 of 7].
subtypes.frequency: Rare subtype of endocervical adenocarcinoma [CERV-A 4 of 7]
subtypes.key_associations: Mesonephric (Wolffian) differentiation; associated with mesonephric remnants; deep lateral wall location; GATA-3 and CD10 positive by IHC [CERV-A 4 of 7]

subtypes.name: Endocervical Adenocarcinoma — HPV-Independent (Endometrioid)
subtypes.description: Endometrioid carcinoma is quite uncommon, approximating 1% of primary endocervical adenocarcinomas, and may present in the setting of endometriosis; however, a primary endometrial carcinoma must be ruled out. Utilizing a panel of IHC stains (vimentin, estrogen receptor [ER], p16, and monoclonal carcinoembryonic antigen [CEA]) may be helpful in differentiating between endocervical and endometrial carcinoma. Typically, endometrioid adenocarcinoma will express vimentin and ER, while endocervical adenocarcinoma is positive for monoclonal CEA and p16 (when HPV-associated) [CERV-A 4 of 7, MS-17].
subtypes.key_associations: May present with endometriosis; expresses vimentin and ER; must differentiate from primary endometrial carcinoma; IHC panel (vimentin, ER, p16, mCEA) useful [CERV-A 4 of 7]

subtypes.name: Adenosquamous Carcinoma (ASC)
subtypes.description: Epithelial tumor with malignant squamous and malignant glandular differentiation. The tumor components (squamous and glandular) should be admixed and be able to be discerned on routine histology. The squamous component typically demonstrates abundant glycogen-rich (clear) cytoplasm, while the gland-forming component is often of usual HPV-associated adenocarcinoma. IHC for p16 usually shows overexpression in both components. Additional IHC stains such as CK7, CEA, and PAX8 may be utilized to highlight the glandular component, while p63 and p40 highlight the squamous component. Clinical outcomes are similar to cervical adenocarcinoma [CERV-A 5 of 7, MS-17].
subtypes.frequency: Approximately 5%–6% of all cervical carcinomas [CERV-A 5 of 7, MS-2]
subtypes.key_associations: Malignant squamous and glandular differentiation; p16 overexpression in both components; similar clinical outcomes to cervical adenocarcinoma [CERV-A 5 of 7]

subtypes.name: Small Cell Neuroendocrine Carcinoma (NECC)
subtypes.description: Although rare, comprising <5% of cervical cancers, the cervix is the most common site for neuroendocrine carcinoma (eg, small cell and large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma) in the genital tract. NECC is clinically aggressive, with rapid metastasis and a frequently poor clinical outcome. NECC is usually HPV-associated; types 16 and 18 are the most common (18 more often than 16). This carcinoma type morphologically resembles neuroendocrine carcinomas of the lung. Small cell NECC is a morphologic diagnosis. The predominant growth pattern is diffuse, with additional patterns including insular, perivascular, and thick trabeculae with serpiginous growth. Cytologic features include a uniform population of cells with indistinct cell borders, scant cytoplasm, and hyperchromatic nuclei with fine granular chromatin. Small cell NECC is variably positive for chromogranin, CD56, and synaptophysin by IHC. CD56 and synaptophysin are the most sensitive neuroendocrine markers, but CD56 lacks specificity. Chromogranin is the most specific neuroendocrine marker, but lacks sensitivity with only about 50%–60% of small cell NECC being positive. Insulinoma-associated protein 1 (INSM1) and synaptophysin are other neuroendocrine markers, with 80% and 70% positivity, respectively. A high percentage of primary NECCs are thyroid transcription factor-1 (TTF-1)-positive, and this marker is of no value in distinction from a pulmonary metastasis [CERV-A 6 of 7, MS-40].
subtypes.frequency: <5% of cervical cancers; accounts for approximately 1%–1.5% of all cervical cancers [CERV-A 6 of 7, MS-40]
subtypes.key_associations: HPV types 16 and 18 (18 > 16); clinically aggressive with rapid metastasis; poor clinical outcome; p16 diffusely positive; TTF-1 positive (cannot distinguish from pulmonary origin); CD56, synaptophysin, chromogranin, INSM1 positive variably [CERV-A 6 of 7, MS-40]

tier_2_handbook.molecular_pathogenesis: The development of cervical cancer is overwhelmingly driven by persistent infection with high-risk human papillomavirus (HPV), which is the most important factor in the development of cervical cancer [MS-2]. The incidence of cervical cancer appears to be related to the prevalence of HPV in the population; in countries with high incidence of cervical cancer, the prevalence of chronic HPV is approximately 10% to 20%, whereas the prevalence in low-incidence countries is 5% to 10% [MS-2]. The majority of cervical SCCs (>90%) are HPV-associated, with high-risk HPV 16 and 18 being the most common types [CERV-A 2 of 7]. Adenocarcinomas of the cervix can also be HPV-associated, with high-risk HPV types 18, 16, and 45 most commonly involved [CERV-A 3 of 7]. Adenosquamous carcinoma accounts for approximately 5% to 6% of all cervical carcinomas, and HPV-associated adenocarcinomas demonstrate block-type p16 expression by IHC in 95% of cases [CERV-A 4 of 7, CERV-A 5 of 7]. Small cell NECC is usually HPV-associated; types 16 and 18 are the most common (18 more often than 16) [CERV-A 6 of 7]. Around 5% of cervical tumors are reported as HPV-independent tumors [MS-2]. HPV-independent cervical SCC, described as a relatively recently entities, have been described to demonstrate TP53, KRAS, ARID1A, and PTEN mutations [CERV-A 2 of 7]. In 2020, the World Health Organization updated the Female Genital Tumors classification of cervical cancer by subdividing the cervical cancer lesions into HPV-associated and HPV-independent tumors based on the new pathological findings [MS-2]. For molecular profiling, the NCCN Panel recommends comprehensive molecular profiling by an FDA-approved assay, or a validated test performed in a CLIA-certified laboratory including at least HER2, mismatch repair (MMR)/microsatellite instability (MSI), tumor mutational burden (TMB) testing, NTRK, and RET for predicting rare pan-tumor targeted therapy opportunities in the setting of metastatic or recurrent disease [CERV-A 1 of 7]. Cervical carcinomas demonstrating HER2 amplification/overexpression have been associated with higher recurrence rates and worse prognosis [CERV-A 1 of 7]. HER2 positivity rate in cervical cancer is approximately 2% to 6% [MS-34]. The HER2-positive rate of HER2 IHC 3+ or 2+ in cervical cancer can be found [MS-34]. The most common HER2 variant in cervical cancer is S310F/Y mutation [MS-16]. NTRK gene fusions are found in about 1% of all solid tumors [MS-15]. RET gene fusions most commonly occur in thyroid and non-small cell lung cancers and are observed in <1% of patients with other solid tumors [MS-16]. TMB testing has shown that cervical cancer had the highest proportion of patients with TMB-high status (21%) in the KEYNOTE-158 study [MS-14]. PD-L1 combined positive score (CPS) testing is recommended for patients with recurrent, progressive, or metastatic disease to guide treatment selection [CERV-A 1 of 7, MS-13]. HPV-independent endocervical adenocarcinomas, including gastric type (10%–15% of cervical adenocarcinomas, associated with Peutz-Jeghers syndrome and STK11 mutation), clear cell (3% of endocervical adenocarcinomas, associated with DES exposure), mesonephric, and endometrioid types, have less favorable prognosis compared to HPV-associated tumors [CERV-A 4 of 7, MS-17].

epidemiology.incidence: An estimated 13,360 new cases of carcinoma of the uterine cervix will be diagnosed in the United States in 2025 [MS-2]. Globally in 2022, there were an estimated 661,021 cervical cancer cases [MS-2]. It is the fourth most common cancer in females worldwide, with 85% of cases occurring in developing countries [MS-2]. It is the most diagnosed cancer in 23 countries and the leading cause of cancer death in 36 countries [MS-2]. Squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) accounts for approximately 80% and adenocarcinoma accounts for approximately 20% of all cervical cancers [MS-2]. The ASC subtype accounts for approximately 5% to 6% of all cervical carcinomas [MS-2]. Small cell NECC accounts for approximately 1% to 1.5% of all cervical cancers [MS-40]. Incidence rates of cervical SCC are highest in Black and Hispanic individuals assigned female at birth, while incidence rates of cervical adenocarcinoma are highest among Hispanic and white individuals assigned female at birth [MS-2]. AC/ASC of the cervix have increased over the past 3 decades [MS-2].
epidemiology.mortality: An estimated 4,320 people are estimated to die of cervical cancer in the United States in 2025 [MS-2]. Globally in 2022, there were 348,189 deaths associated with cervical cancer [MS-2]. Effective treatment for cervical cancer (including surgery and concurrent chemoradiation) can yield cures in 80% of patients with early-stage disease (stages I–II) and in 60% of patients with stage III disease [MS-44].
epidemiology.trends: Cervical cancer rates are decreasing in the United States following the introduction of screening [MS-2]. However, incidence remains high among Hispanic/Latino, Black, and Asian populations [MS-2]. In developed countries, the substantial decline in incidence and mortality of SCC of the cervix is presumed to be the result of effective screening and higher HPV-vaccination coverage, although racial, ethnic, and geographic disparities exist [MS-2]. AC/ASC of the cervix have increased over the past 3 decades, probably because cervical cytologic screening methods are less effective for AC/ASC [MS-2].
epidemiology.demographics: Incidence rates of cervical SCC are highest in Black and Hispanic individuals assigned female at birth, while incidence rates of cervical adenocarcinoma are highest among Hispanic and white individuals assigned female at birth [MS-2]. The substantial decline in incidence and mortality of SCC of the cervix exists alongside racial, ethnic, and geographic disparities [MS-2]. Small cell NECC presents predominantly in younger women (mean age 30–40 years) and is aggressive with rapid metastasis [MS-40].

risk_factors.detail: Persistent HPV infection is the most important factor in the development of cervical cancer. High-risk HPV types 16 and 18 are most commonly implicated, found in the majority of HPV-associated cervical SCCs. In countries with a high incidence of cervical cancer, the prevalence of chronic HPV is approximately 10% to 20%, whereas in low-incidence countries it is 5% to 10%.
risk_factors.strength: Primary etiologic factor — approximately 95% of cervical cancers are HPV-associated [MS-2, CERV-A 1 of 7]

risk_factors.detail: A history of smoking is an epidemiologic risk factor associated with cervical cancer. Smoking cessation should be advised in current smokers, and former smokers should continue to avoid smoking.
Oral contraceptive use has been identified as an epidemiologic risk factor associated with cervical cancer.

risk_factors.factor: Early age of onset of coitus
risk_factors.detail: Early age of onset of sexual intercourse is an epidemiologic risk factor associated with cervical cancer.
A larger number of sexual partners is an epidemiologic risk factor associated with cervical cancer.
A history of sexually transmitted disease is an epidemiologic risk factor associated with cervical cancer.
Certain autoimmune diseases have been identified as epidemiologic risk factors associated with cervical cancer.

Chronic immunosuppression, including HIV infection, is an epidemiologic risk factor associated with cervical cancer. The guidelines recommend considering HIV testing, especially in younger patients. Patients with cervical cancer and HIV should be referred to an HIV specialist and should be treated for cervical cancer as per the guidelines without modification solely on the basis of HIV status.
risk_factors.strength: Established risk factor [MS-2, CERV-1 footnote d]

protective_factors: HPV vaccination — Immunization against HPV prevents infection with the types of HPV against which the vaccine is designed and is expected to prevent specific HPV cancer. HPV vaccines may also decrease the incidence of both squamous cell carcinoma and adenocarcinoma [MS-2].

Effective cervical cancer screening (cytology and/or HPV testing) — The substantial decline in incidence and mortality of SCC of the cervix in developed countries is presumed to be the result of effective screening [MS-2].
Smoking cessation and abstinence — Smoking cessation should be advised in current smokers, and former smokers should continue to avoid smoking [MS-3].

clinical_features.typical_presentation: Cervical cancer may present in early stages as asymptomatic or with subtle symptoms such as watery vaginal discharge, postcoital bleeding, or intermittent spotting, which are often not recognized by the patient. As the disease advances, symptoms can include pelvic pain, leg edema, weight loss, anorexia, and persistent coughing. On examination, a visible or palpable cervical mass, ulceration, or barrel-shaped cervix may be noted. For advanced stages, involvement of adjacent organs (e.g., bladder, rectum) can cause hematuria, dysuria, rectal bleeding, or constipation.

symptoms.name: Pelvic pain
symptoms.detail: Dull, aching pain in the pelvis, lower back, or legs

symptoms.name: Leg edema
symptoms.detail: Unilateral or bilateral swelling due to lymphatic or venous obstruction

signs.name: Cervical mass or ulcer
signs.detail: Visible or palpable lesion on pelvic examination

signs.name: Barrel-shaped cervix
signs.detail: Enlarged, firm cervix suggesting deep stromal invasion

signs.name: Parametrial induration
signs.detail: Fixation or thickening of the parametria on bimanual exam

signs.name: Pelvic sidewall mass
signs.detail: Fixed mass extending to the pelvic wall

atypical_or_missed: Early-stage disease may be entirely asymptomatic and discovered only via screening Pap tests or HPV testing.
Adenocarcinomas are located deeper in the endocervical canal and may be missed by cytologic screening, leading to delayed diagnosis.
Small cell neuroendocrine carcinoma (NECC) can present aggressively with rapid metastasis and may mimic other small cell carcinomas (e.g., lung primary).

red_flags: Postcoital or unexplained vaginal bleeding (especially in postmenopausal women)
Pelvic pain with or without a palpable mass
Barrel-shaped cervix or fixed pelvic mass on examination
Hematuria, rectal bleeding, or bowel obstruction symptoms
Unintentional weight loss or persistent cough
Leg edema or lymphedema suggesting advanced disease

diagnostic.rationale: Gold standard for diagnosis of cervical cancer; obtains tissue for histologic subtype and grade.
diagnostic.key_findings: Invasive squamous cell carcinoma, adenocarcinoma, adenosquamous carcinoma, or small cell neuroendocrine carcinoma.

diagnostic.test: Cone biopsy (cold knife conization preferred, LEEP acceptable)
diagnostic.rationale: Required to assess depth of invasion, margin status, and lymphovascular space invasion (LVSI) for staging and treatment planning.
diagnostic.key_findings: Depth of invasion in mm, tumor width, margin status, LVSI, and histologic pattern (e.g., Silva classification for adenocarcinoma).

diagnostic.rationale: Evaluates endocervical canal for invasion, especially when adenocarcinoma in situ vs. invasive adenocarcinoma is suspected.

staging.rationale: Preferred imaging for assessing local tumor extent, parametrial invasion, vaginal involvement, and lymph node status.
staging.indication: All stages, especially for fertility-sparing surgery planning and radiation treatment planning.

staging.rationale: Preferred for evaluating metastatic disease (lymph nodes, distant metastases) in stages IB1–IVA.
staging.indication: Stages IB1–IVA; also for post-treatment surveillance if high-risk factors.

staging.rationale: Alternative to PET/CT for metastatic evaluation; useful for assessing lymph nodes and distant organs.
staging.indication: When PET/CT is not available or for initial workup in some stages.

staging.rationale: May be used if MRI is contraindicated to assess local tumor extent.
staging.indication: Fertility-sparing workup when MRI is unavailable.

staging.rationale: For small cell neuroendocrine carcinoma (NECC) to evaluate for brain metastases.
To evaluate bladder or rectal invasion when symptoms or clinical findings suggest involvement (e.g., barrel-shaped lesion, anterior vaginal involvement).
Allows thorough pelvic assessment for parametrial and vaginal involvement.
staging.indication: When clinical exam is limited or for precise staging.

biomarkers.rationale: Predicts response to pembrolizumab in recurrent/metastatic disease.
biomarkers.when: All patients with recurrent, progressive, or metastatic cervical cancer.

biomarkers.test: HPV testing (in situ hybridization or molecular testing) or p16 IHC
biomarkers.rationale: Determines HPV association; HPV-associated tumors have better prognosis and may guide treatment.
biomarkers.when: All cervical adenocarcinomas and when histologic subtype is uncertain.

biomarkers.test: HER2 IHC (with reflex to FISH if equivocal)
biomarkers.rationale: Identifies HER2 amplification/overexpression, which is associated with worse prognosis and potential targeted therapy (e.g., fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan).
biomarkers.when: Advanced, metastatic, or recurrent cervical carcinoma.

biomarkers.rationale: dMMR/MSI-H tumors may respond to pembrolizumab.
TMB-high tumors (≥10 mut/Mb) may respond to pembrolizumab.
Identifies tumors with NTRK fusions, which are sensitive to TRK inhibitors (larotrectinib, entrectinib, repotrectinib).
Identifies tumors with RET fusions, which are sensitive to selpercatinib.

do_not_miss: FDG-PET/CT or chest/abdomen/pelvis CT for metastatic evaluation
avoid: Routine cytology (Pap test) for surveillance of recurrent disease (limited value, especially after pelvic radiation).
CT of the abdomen/pelvis without contrast for initial staging (insufficient for soft tissue detail).
Surgical staging (e.g., laparoscopic para-aortic lymphadenectomy) is not routinely recommended for all patients; imaging is preferred initially.

staging.system: FIGO 2018 staging system (International Federation of Gynecology and Obstetrics)

staging_pearls: Staging is clinical based on FIGO 2018, but imaging (r) and pathology (p) findings can supplement staging, especially for lymph node status (stage IIIC).
Lymphovascular space invasion (LVSI) does not alter the FIGO stage but is an important prognostic factor for adjuvant treatment decisions.
For small cell neuroendocrine carcinoma (NECC), the same FIGO staging system is used, but the tumor is highly aggressive with frequent early metastasis.
In adenocarcinomas, the Silva pattern of invasion may provide additional prognostic information beyond depth of invasion.
Isolated tumor cells in lymph nodes do not change the stage but should be recorded.

management_principles.overview: The primary goal of cervical cancer treatment is curative intent, achieved through a multidisciplinary approach tailored to disease stage, histology (squamous cell carcinoma, adenocarcinoma, adenosquamous carcinoma), and patient factors. Treatment philosophy is guided by the 2018 FIGO staging system and involves a risk-stratified strategy. For early-stage disease, the primary modality is surgery, often with fertility-sparing options considered for select patients. For locally advanced disease (stages IB3-IVA), definitive concurrent chemoradiation is the standard of care. For recurrent or metastatic disease, systemic therapy incorporating targeted agents and immunotherapy is central. Management of the rare small cell neuroendocrine carcinoma (NECC) of the cervix is modeled after regimens for small cell lung cancer. The NCCN emphasizes the importance of clinical trials and shared decision-making [1].

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with localized or locoregional disease (FIGO stages IA-IVA without distant metastases).
by_intent.approach: Treatment is directed at eliminating all detectable disease. Primary options include surgery (cone biopsy, trachelectomy, hysterectomy) for early stages, and definitive chemoradiation for locally advanced stages. Adjuvant therapy (radiation or chemoradiation) may be added postoperatively based on pathologic risk factors.

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with recurrent, persistent, or metastatic disease not amenable to curative therapy (e.g., Stage IVB, distant metastases).
by_intent.approach: Treatment aims to control symptoms, prolong survival, and improve quality of life. Primary modalities include systemic therapy (chemotherapy, immunotherapy, targeted agents). Local therapies (e.g., radiation, ablative procedures) may be used for symptom control or oligometastatic disease [1].

management_principles.mdt_requirements: Management requires a multidisciplinary team (MDT) including gynecologic oncologists, radiation oncologists, medical oncologists, pathologists, radiologists, and fertility specialists (REI). The NCCN panel is composed of experts from these disciplines. Complex cases, such as fertility preservation or pelvic exenteration for recurrence, should be managed at specialized centers [1].
management_principles.performance_status_guide: While not explicitly detailed with ECOG/PS cutoffs in this excerpt, the guideline implies that treatment intensity is tailored to patient fitness. For example, concurrent chemoradiation is standard for advanced disease but requires adequate organ function. Surgical candidacy is assessed medically. Systemic therapy options, including intensive combination regimens, are generally considered for patients with good performance status. Supportive care and best supportive care are options for those not tolerating active therapy [1].

items.text: Complete history and physical examination
Assess for distress (use NCCN Distress Thermometer)
Complete blood count (CBC) including platelets
Liver function test (LFT)
Renal function tests (BUN, creatinine)
Consider HIV testing, especially in younger patients
Cervical biopsy with pathologic review (Principles of Pathology CERV-A)
Cone biopsy (CKC preferred, LEEP acceptable) as indicated for staging/treatment
Histologic type (HPV-associated vs. HPV-independent), grade, stromal invasion, LVSI, margin status
PD-L1 testing by FDA-approved assay for recurrent, progressive, or metastatic disease
HER2 IHC testing (with reflex to FISH for equivocal) for advanced, metastatic, or recurrent carcinoma
Comprehensive molecular profiling (including MMR/MSI, TMB, NTRK, RET) by FDA-approved/CLIA-certified assay for metastatic or recurrent disease
Pelvic MRI to assess local disease extent (preferred for Stage IB1-IB3, and when considering conservative surgery)
FDG-PET/CT (preferred) or chest/abdomen/pelvis CT or FDG-PET/MRI to evaluate for metastatic disease in Stage IB1-IB3 and ≥Stage II
items.recommendation: Preferred for Stage IB1-IB3 and ≥Stage II (CERV-B)

items.text: For small cell NECC: Neck/chest/abdomen/pelvis/groin FDG-PET/CT + brain MRI (preferred) or Chest/abdomen/pelvis CT + brain MRI
Imaging should be based on symptomatology and clinical concern for metastatic disease
Consider examination under anesthesia (EUA) cystoscopy/proctoscopy (based on symptoms, barrel-shaped lesions, or anterior vaginal involvement)
Consider options for fertility sparing or referral to reproductive endocrinology and infertility (REI) specialist
Smoking cessation and counseling intervention
Cytology is not recommended for routine surveillance of recurrent cervical cancer, as its value is limited
Cytology is not recommended in patients with prior radiation, given limited accuracy

surgery.role: Primary treatment for early-stage disease (stages IA, IB1, IB2, select IIA1) with curative intent. Also used for fertility preservation in select patients. Salvage surgery (pelvic exenteration) is an option for central pelvic recurrence after prior RT. Surgical staging via lymphadenectomy informs adjuvant therapy.
principles: Treatment is stratified by stage and histology (CERV 1-5).

Fertility-sparing approaches (conization, trachelectomy) are options for highly selected patients with early-stage, low-risk disease (Stage IA1 with no LVSI, Stage IA2-IB1 meeting conservative criteria). Consultation with REI experts is suggested.

Querleu and Morrow classification (Type A, B, C) describes the extent of hysterectomy. Open abdominal approach is standard (Category 1). Minimally invasive radical hysterectomy is associated with lower DFS and OS in a randomized trial (LACC).
Sentinel lymph node (SLN) mapping is recommended for early-stage disease; best detection in tumors <2 cm. Adherence to the SLN algorithm (side-specific dissection for failed mapping) is critical.
Para-aortic lymphadenectomy is recommended for ≥Stage IB1 disease.
Pelvic exenteration for recurrence should be performed at high-volume centers.

procedures.name: Cone Biopsy (Conization)
procedures.approach: Cold knife conization (CKC) preferred; LEEP acceptable if margins/orientation adequate.
procedures.indications: Diagnostic staging for microinvasive disease; fertility-sparing treatment for Stage IA1 (no LVSI); part of conservative surgery for Stage IA2-IB1 meeting criteria.
procedures.key_complications: Hemorrhage, infection, cervical stenosis, preterm delivery in future pregnancies.

procedures.name: Type A Hysterectomy (Simple/Extrafascial)
procedures.indications: Non-fertility-sparing treatment for Stage IA1 (no LVSI, negative margins); treatment for Stage IA2-IB1 meeting all conservative surgery criteria (CERV-4).
procedures.key_complications: Hemorrhage, infection, urinary/rectal injury, vaginal cuff dehiscence.

procedures.name: Type B Radical Hysterectomy (Modified Radical)
procedures.indications: Stage IA1 with LVSI and positive carcinoma margins; some cases of Stage IA2-IB1.
procedures.key_complications: Increased risk of urinary retention, ureteral injury, lymphedema compared to Type A.

procedures.name: Type C1 Radical Hysterectomy
procedures.indications: Stage IB1 (not meeting conservative criteria), IB2, IIA1 (Category 1). Nerve-sparing.
procedures.key_complications: Urinary retention, bladder dysfunction, lymphedema, sexual dysfunction.

procedures.name: Radical Trachelectomy (Vaginal or Abdominal)
procedures.indications: Fertility-sparing for Stage IB1 (not meeting conservative criteria), select IB2 (tumors <2 cm).
procedures.key_complications: Cervical stenosis, incompetence, preterm birth, need for adjuvant therapy.

procedures.name: Pelvic Exenteration (Anterior, Posterior, Total)
procedures.indications: Central pelvic recurrence after prior RT, no distant metastasis.
procedures.key_complications: High morbidity (ureteral complications, fistula, infection), psychosocial impact.

surgery.resectability_criteria: Surgical candidacy is determined by medical operability, disease extent (stage, absence of distant metastases), and patient factors. For fertility-sparing surgery, strict criteria must be met: tumor size ≤2 cm, no LVSI (preferred), negative margins, specific histology (squamous any grade, usual type adenocarcinoma G1-2), negative imaging for locoregional disease.
surgery.pre_operative_optimisation: General pre-operative assessment and optimization of comorbidities. Consider fertility counseling and REI referral for candidates for fertility-sparing surgery. Smoking cessation is advised.

radiation_therapy.role: Definitive treatment for locally advanced disease (Stage IB3-IVA) and medically inoperable early-stage disease. Adjuvant treatment after hysterectomy for intermediate-risk (GOG/Sedlis criteria) and high-risk (positive nodes, margins, parametrium) features. Palliative treatment for recurrent/metastatic disease.
principles: CT-based treatment planning with conformal blocking is standard. MRI is best for soft tissue assessment; FDG-PET/CT is useful for nodal volume definition.
IMRT is preferred to minimize toxicities in definitive and post-hysterectomy settings.
Brachytherapy is a critical component of definitive RT for intact cervix. Image-guided adaptive brachytherapy (IGABT) with MRI improves outcomes.
Concurrent platinum-containing chemotherapy (cisplatin, or carboplatin if cisplatin intolerant) is standard with definitive EBRT for locally advanced disease.
Optimal results when treatment is completed within 8 weeks.
SBRT is not an appropriate routine alternative to brachytherapy.
Reirradiation (IORT, brachytherapy, SBRT, IMRT, proton) is highly customized.

dose_frameworks.name: Whole Pelvic EBRT (Microscopic Disease)
dose_frameworks.indication: Initial treatment for definitive RT (intact cervix) or adjuvant RT post-hysterectomy.

dose_frameworks.name: Boost to Gross Nodes (SIB with IMRT)
Brachytherapy (Point A Dose, LDR Equivalent)
dose_frameworks.indication: Boost for intact cervix after EBRT.

dose_frameworks.name: Brachytherapy (HR-CTV D90, EQD2)
dose_frameworks.total_dose: ≥85 Gy (preferred); 75-80 Gy for very small tumors

dose_frameworks.name: Post-Hysterectomy Vaginal Cuff Boost
dose_frameworks.indication: Positive or close vaginal margins after radical hysterectomy, or after total/simple hysterectomy if completion upper vaginectomy not performed.

approaches.name: Definitive Chemoradiation (Intact Cervix)
approaches.dose_fractionation: EBRT 45 Gy + concurrent cisplatin ± pembrolizumab, followed by brachytherapy to HR-CTV D90 ≥85 Gy.
approaches.key_trial: KEYNOTE-A18 (Pembrolizumab added for FIGO 2014 stage IIIA-IIIA).
approaches.toxicities: Acute: Cystitis, proctitis, diarrhea, fatigue, hematologic toxicity. Late: Fistula, stenosis, bowel/bladder injury.

approaches.name: Adjuvant Radiation (Post-Hysterectomy)
approaches.dose_fractionation: EBRT 45-50 Gy to pelvis ± vaginal cuff boost.
approaches.indication: High-risk (positive nodes, margins, parametrium) or intermediate-risk (meeting GOG/Sedlis criteria) disease.

approaches.name: Adjuvant Extended-Field Radiation
approaches.dose_fractionation: EBRT to pelvis and para-aorta to L3/L4 level, 45 Gy.
approaches.toxicities: Increased risk of renal, bowel, and hematologic toxicity.

approaches.name: Reirradiation
approaches.dose_fractionation: Highly individualized (IORT, SBRT, IMRT, brachytherapy, proton).
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Variable, based on prior therapy and tolerance.
approaches.toxicities: High risk of severe late toxicity (fistula, necrosis).

systemic_therapy.overview: Systemic therapy is used concurrently with radiation for locally advanced disease, as adjuvant therapy, and as primary treatment for recurrent/metastatic disease. The landscape includes traditional cytotoxic chemotherapy, anti-angiogenic agents (bevacizumab), immune checkpoint inhibitors (pembrolizumab, cemiplimab, atezolizumab, nivolumab), and antibody-drug conjugates (tisotumab vedotin). Treatment selection is increasingly guided by biomarkers (PD-L1, MSI-H/dMMR, TMB-H, HER2, NTRK, RET).

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Concurrent with Definitive EBRT (Locally Advanced Disease)
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Cisplatin ± Pembrolizumab (Category 1 for FIGO 2014 stage IIIA, IIIB, IVA; Category 2B for select FIGO 2018 stage III-IVA). Carboplatin ± Pembrolizumab if cisplatin intolerant.
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Category 1 evidence from randomized trials showing improved survival with concurrent cisplatin over RT alone. KEYNOTE-A18 adds pembrolizumab for improved PFS/OS in stage III-IVA.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: If single-agent cisplatin/carboplatin unavailable: Capecitabine/Mitomycin, Gemcitabine, Paclitaxel. Induction Carboplatin/Paclitaxel followed by chemoradiation (INTERLACE protocol) can be considered.
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: PD-L1 testing recommended for pembrolizumab eligibility.

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Pelvic EBRT + concurrent cisplatin (or carboplatin if cisplatin intolerant) ± brachytherapy.
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Category 1 evidence (GOG 109) for improved survival with concurrent chemoradiation in high-risk disease (positive nodes/margins/parametrium).
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Capecitabine/Mitomycin, Gemcitabine, Paclitaxel as radiosensitizers if platinum unavailable.

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: For PD-L1-positive tumors (CPS ≥1): Cisplatin/Paclitaxel + Pembrolizumab ± Bevacizumab (Category 1) OR Carboplatin/Paclitaxel + Pembrolizumab ± Bevacizumab (Category 1). Cisplatin/Paclitaxel + Bevacizumab (Category 1) OR Carboplatin/Paclitaxel + Bevacizumab. Cisplatin/Paclitaxel + Atezolizumab + Bevacizumab (Category 1) OR Carboplatin/Paclitaxel + Atezolizumab + Bevacizumab (Category 1).
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: KEYNOTE-826 (pembrolizumab) and BEATcc (atezolizumab) trials demonstrated significant OS/PFS benefits with addition of IO to chemo ± bevacizumab in first-line setting.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Cisplatin/Paclitaxel (Category 1), Carboplatin/Paclitaxel (Category 1; Category 1 for prior cisplatin), Paclitaxel/Topotecan + Bevacizumab (Category 1).

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: TMB-H tumors: Pembrolizumab. PD-L1-positive: Pembrolizumab. MSI-H/dMMR tumors: Pembrolizumab. Tisotumab vedotin-tftv (Category 1).
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Pembrolizumab approved for TMB-H, PD-L1+, MSI-H/dMMR tumors. Tisotumab vedotin showed OS benefit over chemotherapy in innovaTV-301 trial.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Bevacizumab, Paclitaxel, Albumin-bound Paclitaxel, Docetaxel, Fluorouracil, Gemcitabine, Pemetrexed, Topotecan, Vinorelbine, Irinotecan, Cemiplimab, Ipilimumab + Nivolumab.
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: TMB-H (≥10 mut/Mb), PD-L1, MSI-H/dMMR by FDA-approved assay.

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Recurrent or Metastatic Disease - Biomarker-Directed (Useful in Certain Circumstances)
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: HER2-positive (IHC 3+ or 2+): Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki. HER2-mutant: Neratinib. RET gene fusion-positive: Selpercatinib. NTRK gene fusion-positive: Larotrectinib, Entrectinib, Repotrectinib. PD-L1-positive: Nivolumab; Pembrolizumab + Tisotumab vedotin-tftv.
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Tumor-agnostic approvals based on high response rates in basket trials (DESTINY-PanTumor02, SUMMIT, LIBRETTO-001, STARTRK, TRIDENT-1, CheckMate-358, innovaTV 205).
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: HER2, RET, NTRK, PD-L1 testing by validated assay.

key_regimens.name: Cisplatin/Paclitaxel + Pembrolizumab ± Bevacizumab
key_regimens.setting: First-line for recurrent/metastatic, PD-L1-positive (CPS ≥1) cervical cancer.
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Neutropenia, anemia, thrombocytopenia, fatigue, alopecia, neuropathy, nausea, hypertension, proteinuria, immune-mediated adverse reactions.

key_regimens.name: Tisotumab Vedotin-tftv
key_regimens.setting: Second-line or subsequent therapy for recurrent/metastatic cervical cancer (Category 1).
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Hemorrhage, conjunctival adverse events, fatigue, nausea, diarrhea, epistaxis, dry eye.

key_regimens.name: Cisplatin/Etoposide + Radiation (for Small Cell NECC)
key_regimens.setting: Concurrent chemoradiation for locally advanced small cell NECC.
key_regimens.cycle_days: Concurrent with RT (first two cycles on Days 1,22; two cycles after RT)

treatment_response_assessment.title: Response Assessment and Biopsy Logic
treatment_response_assessment.timing: Post-treatment surveillance is based on risk of recurrence. High-risk patients are assessed more frequently (every 3 months for 2 years). Imaging within 3-6 months post-chemoradiation for stage II-IVA. For small cell NECC, reassessment after neoadjuvant chemotherapy is recommended.
response_logic: All recurrences should be proven by biopsy before proceeding to treatment planning for recurrent disease.
For suspected recurrence or metastasis, perform neck/chest/abdomen/pelvis/groin FDG-PET/CT and consider pelvis MRI.

For small cell NECC, if primary treatment is chemoradiation, FDG-PET/CT ± brain MRI is preferred for assessment. If neoadjuvant chemotherapy is used, reassessment prior to chemoradiation/brachytherapy is recommended.
imaging_recommendations: Surveillance imaging is stage-dependent. For Stage I: based on symptoms/concern. For Stage II-IV: FDG-PET/CT preferred within 3-6 months post-therapy.
If first post-treatment FDG-PET/CT is indeterminate, consider repeating in 3 months.
For fertility-sparing surgery: Consider pelvic MRI 6 months post-surgery and yearly for 2-3 years.
For small cell NECC surveillance: FDG-PET/CT ± brain MRI preferred.
biopsy_or_salvage_logic: Biopsy of suspicious areas is indicated to confirm recurrence/metastasis before systemic therapy.
For central pelvic recurrence after prior RT, evaluate for pelvic exenteration (with or without IORT) in carefully selected patients.
For recurrent small cell NECC with local disease, consider pelvic exenteration if feasible.
For oligometastatic disease, consider local ablative therapies (resection, SBRT) ± systemic therapy.

prognosis.overall: Cervical cancer prognosis varies significantly by stage at diagnosis, with 5-year survival rates ranging from >90% for stage I disease to <20% for distant metastatic disease. The 5-year relative survival rate for all localized cervical cancer is approximately 92%, while for regional disease it is 60%, and for distant metastases it is only 19% [1,2]. The overall 5-year relative survival rate is approximately 67% [2].

by_stage.context: Excellent prognosis with <1% risk of lymphatic metastasis. Treatment with conization or simple hysterectomy is curative in nearly all cases [3,4].
Excellent prognosis with treatment. Radical hysterectomy or radiation therapy provides excellent local control. Fertility-sparing approaches are safe in select cases meeting specific criteria [5,6].
Good prognosis. Treatment typically involves radical hysterectomy or definitive chemoradiation, with outcomes dependent on surgical/pathologic risk factors [5,6].
Prognosis is primarily driven by response to definitive chemoradiation, which is the standard of care. Adding pembrolizumab to chemoradiation for FIGO 2014 stage III–IVA improves survival [7].

Prognosis is dependent on response to definitive chemoradiation. Concurrent platinum-based chemoradiation is standard. The addition of pembrolizumab improves PFS and OS in FIGO 2014 stage III–IVA disease [7,8].

by_stage.stage: Stage IVB or Recurrence with Distant Metastases
by_stage.context: Poor prognosis. Treatment is palliative with systemic therapy. Long-term survival is rare, though isolated oligometastatic disease may be amenable to local therapy with systemic treatment [9].

prognostic_factors: Lymph node status (positive nodes confer poorer prognosis) [10]
Tumor size and depth of invasion [11]
Histologic subtype (e.g., small cell neuroendocrine carcinoma is aggressive with poor prognosis) [13]
HPV status (HPV-independent tumors may have worse prognosis) [14]
Surgical margin status (positive margins increase recurrence risk) [15]
prognosis.prognostic_context: Prognosis is stage-dependent, with early-stage disease (FIGO 2018 stage I) having excellent survival due to effective local therapies. The main determinants of outcome in early-stage disease are lymph node metastasis and high-risk pathologic features (positive margins, parametrial invasion) prompting adjuvant therapy. For locally advanced disease (stage IB3–IVA), prognosis hinges on the effectiveness of definitive chemoradiation, with the addition of immunotherapy (pembrolizumab) improving outcomes in select patients. In metastatic or recurrent disease, prognosis is generally poor, but biomarker-directed therapies (PD-L1, TMB-H, MSI-H, NTRK, RET, HER2) offer meaningful benefit in subsets of patients.

disease_related.management: May occur with stage IIIB disease due to pelvic sidewall extension. Management may include ureteral stenting or nephrostomy as palliative measures or to preserve renal function during treatment [17].
Rare at diagnosis but can be a complication of advanced disease or treatment. May require surgical repair (e.g., exenteration) or diversion procedures [18].
Risk after pelvic lymphadenectomy. Managed with compression garments, manual lymphatic drainage, and exercise. Sentinel lymph node mapping may reduce risk [19,20].

chemotherapy.management: G-CSF prophylaxis may be considered, especially with regimens with high risk (>20%) of febrile neutropenia. Dose reductions or delays may be necessary [21].
Vigorous pre- and post-hydration with normal saline is standard. Monitor renal function (BUN, creatinine). Carboplatin is an alternative for cisplatin-intolerant patients [22].
Monitor for symptoms. Dose reduction or discontinuation may be required. No standard prevention; duloxetine may offer some symptomatic relief for paclitaxel-induced neuropathy [22,23].
High emetogenic risk with cisplatin; moderate with carboplatin. Follow NCCN Antiemesis Guidelines. Dexamethasone + 5-HT3 antagonist + NK1 receptor antagonist is typical for cisplatin [24].

Common with paclitaxel and platinum agents. For mild reactions, premedication with steroids/antihistamines. For severe reactions, the implicated agent should be avoided unless evaluated by a specialist for desensitization [25,26].

immunotherapy.management: Can affect any organ system. Key toxicities include colitis, hepatitis, pneumonitis, endocrinopathies, and dermatologic reactions. Management follows NCCN Guidelines for Management of Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor-Related Toxicities (immunotherapy holds, corticosteroids, and other immunosuppressants as needed) [7,27].

targeted_therapy.complication: Bevacizumab-related hypertension, proteinuria, thrombosis, GI perforation
targeted_therapy.management: Monitor blood pressure and urine protein. Withhold for grade 3+ hypertension or proteinuria. Educate patients on signs/symptoms of thromboembolic events and GI perforation [28].
Proactive eye care with lubricant drops, steroid eye drops, cold compresses, and wearing eye protection. Ophthalmology consultation is recommended [29,30].

surgery.management: Intraoperative repair. Postoperative complications like fistulae may require delayed repair or diversion [31].
Lymphocyst: may resolve spontaneously or require drainage. Lymphedema: as above [19,20].
Standard perioperative antibiotic prophylaxis and VTE prophylaxis [32].

radiation.management: Symptomatic management (antispasmodics, stool softeners, topical agents). IMRT reduces GI and GU toxicity compared to conventional RT [33,34].

radiation.complication: Late: Fibrosis, vaginal stenosis, fistulae, bowel obstruction
radiation.management: Preventive: Vaginal dilator use, lubricants. Management: Surgical repair for fistulae/obstruction; hyperbaric oxygen for select cases of radiation necrosis [33,35].
Long-term surveillance for cancers in radiation fields (e.g., colon, bladder) [36].

supportive_care.overview: Comprehensive supportive care is integral to managing cervical cancer patients, addressing treatment toxicities, nutritional status, psychosocial well-being, and survivorship concerns. Coordination between oncology, primary care, and specialists is essential [37].
supportive_care.nutritional_support: Assess nutritional status at each visit. Referral to a dietitian is recommended for weight loss >5%, poor oral intake, or malnutrition. Nutritional counseling and support to maintain adequate caloric and protein intake are important, especially during chemoradiation when mucositis and diarrhea may impair intake [37,38].
supportive_care.anti_emetic_protocol: Follow NCCN Antiemesis Guidelines. High emetogenic chemotherapy (HEC: cisplatin, >24h infusions of carboplatin) warrants a 3-drug regimen: NK1 receptor antagonist + 5-HT3 antagonist + dexamethasone. Moderately emetogenic chemotherapy (MEC: carboplatin AUC <4, paclitaxel, docetaxel) typically uses 5-HT3 antagonist + dexamethasone. Delayed nausea prophylaxis with dexamethasone may be needed for HEC [24].
supportive_care.gcsf_guidance: Primary G-CSF prophylaxis is recommended for regimens with a high risk (≥20%) of febrile neutropenia (e.g., dose-dense paclitaxel, topotecan, or combination regimens like cisplatin/etoposide for NECC). For intermediate risk regimens (10-20%), consider patient-specific risk factors (age, comorbidities, prior episodes). Use of biosimilar filgrastim or pegfilgrastim is acceptable [21].
supportive_care.vte_prophylaxis: VTE prophylaxis is recommended for hospitalized patients and for patients receiving anti-angiogenic agents (bevacizumab). Consider pharmacologic prophylaxis (LMWH or DOACs) for high-risk ambulatory patients with cancer, particularly during periods of immobilization or with combination chemotherapy [32].
supportive_care.pain_management: Assess pain at every visit. Use WHO analgesic ladder. Neuropathic pain (from platinum/taxanes or radiation plexopathy) may require agents like gabapentin, pregabalin, or duloxetine. For bone metastases, consider bone-modifying agents (zoledronic acid, denosumab) and palliative radiation. Refer to pain or palliative care specialists for complex pain [37,39].
supportive_care.psychosocial_support: Screen for distress using the NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List. Provide referrals for psychosocial support (psychotherapy, support groups), financial counseling, and fertility preservation counseling as appropriate. Address survivorship concerns including body image, sexual health, and fear of recurrence [37,40].
supportive_care.dental_care: Refer for dental evaluation and necessary treatment before starting chemoradiation to mitigate osteonecrosis risk, especially if mandible/maxilla will be in radiation fields. Recommend fluoride trays and regular dental care during and after RT [37,41].

follow_up.post_curative_treatment: After completion of curative-intent treatment, follow-up is based on the Society of Gynecologic Oncology (SGO) recommendations, tailored to recurrence risk and patient preference [42].
follow_up.surveillance_rationale: The primary goal of surveillance is the early detection of recurrent or metastatic disease, as some recurrences may be amenable to curative-intent salvage therapy. Patient education about symptoms of recurrence (e.g., vaginal discharge, bleeding, pain) is emphasized. While routine imaging and cytology have limited value in detecting asymptomatic recurrences, they are used selectively based on stage and risk [42,43].
late_effects_screening: Screen for genitourinary symptoms: dysuria, frequency, incontinence [44]
Screen for gastrointestinal symptoms: chronic diarrhea, rectal bleeding [44]
Assess for sexual dysfunction: vaginal dryness, stenosis, dyspareunia [44,45]
Evaluate for lymphedema of lower extremities [44]
Screen for bone density loss, especially post-oophorectomy or pelvic RT [44]
Assess for psychosocial issues: depression, anxiety, fear of recurrence [40]
Provide counseling on healthy lifestyle, smoking cessation, and obesity management [44]
Discuss second cancer risks (e.g., colon, bladder) relevant to prior RT fields [36]
follow_up.recurrence_patterns: Most recurrences occur within the first 2-3 years post-treatment. Patterns include: 1) Local/pelvic recurrence (most common), 2) Distant metastases (e.g., lungs, liver, bone, para-aortic nodes), 3) Combined local and distant recurrence. The location of recurrence influences salvage therapy options (e.g., pelvic exenteration for central pelvic recurrence) [46,47].

clinical_follow_up_schedule: History & Physical Exam: Every 3–6 months for the first 2 years, then every 6–12 months for years 3–5, and annually thereafter [42].
More frequent visits (e.g., every 3 months for 2 years) may be considered for high-risk disease [42].
Patient education on symptoms of recurrence (e.g., vaginal discharge, bleeding, pain) at each visit [42].
imaging_strategy: Stage I disease: Imaging based on symptoms or clinical concern. For fertility-sparing treatment, consider pelvic MRI 6 months after surgery and yearly for 2–3 years [42,48].

Stage II–IV disease: Consider neck/chest/abdomen/pelvis/groin FDG-PET/CT (preferred) or chest/abdomen/pelvis CT within 3–6 months of completing therapy, especially for high-risk features (e.g., positive nodes, parametrial involvement). Consider pelvis MRI at 3–6 months post-therapy [48,49].
Stage IVB or Recurrence: Imaging as appropriate (CT, MRI, FDG-PET/CT) to assess response or guide further therapy [48].
Biopsy proven recurrence is recommended before salvage therapy [42].
laboratory_monitoring: Complete Blood Count (CBC): As clinically indicated (e.g., during chemotherapy or if symptoms arise) [42].
Renal Function (BUN/Creatinine): As clinically indicated, especially with prior cisplatin therapy or impaired renal function [42].

Cervical/vaginal cytology: Has limited value in detecting recurrence. May be considered for surveillance of lower genital tract dysplasia, particularly after fertility-sparing surgery. Not recommended post-pelvic radiation due to low accuracy [42,50].
Tumor markers: Not routinely recommended. No established serum marker for cervical cancer [42].
supportive_follow_up: Assessment and management of treatment-related late effects (see "late_effects_screening") [44].
Counseling on sexual health, including use of vaginal dilators, lubricants/moisturizers, and local estrogen therapy for menopause [45].
Referral to Survivorship program as per Principles of Gynecologic Survivorship [37].

special_situations.situation: Incidental Finding of Invasive Cervical Cancer After Simple/Total Hysterectomy
special_situations.key_message: Management is determined by final pathologic findings (stage, histology, margins, LVSI) and requires imaging for staging.
special_situations.management: For stage IA1 with no LVSI and negative margins: Surveillance. For IA1 with LVSI or IA2–IB1 meeting conservative criteria: Consider second surgery with pelvic lymphadenectomy ± adjuvant therapy based on nodal status. For positive margins or higher-risk features: Pelvic EBRT ± brachytherapy ± concurrent platinum-based chemotherapy [51,52].
special_situations.evidence: Treatment is adapted from primary therapy guidelines based on pathologic stage. No randomized trials specific to this scenario; recommendations extrapolated from primary treatment data [51].

special_situations.key_message: Requires multidisciplinary care balancing maternal treatment with fetal maturity.
special_situations.management: For early-stage disease (IA1–IB1): May consider delaying definitive treatment (e.g., cesarean hysterectomy) until fetal maturity if diagnosis is in the third trimester. Radical trachelectomy has been performed in select cases. For advanced disease: Treatment is not delayed, which may result in pregnancy termination [53,54].
special_situations.evidence: Limited data, mostly from case series. Conclusions are based on expert consensus and retrospective analyses [53].

special_situations.situation: Small Cell Neuroendocrine Carcinoma of the Cervix (NECC)
special_situations.key_message: Aggressive histology requiring multimodal treatment akin to small cell lung cancer.
special_situations.management: For stage IA1–IB2 (≤4 cm): Radical hysterectomy + SLN mapping/pelvic lymphadenectomy followed by adjuvant chemotherapy (cisplatin/etoposide) or chemoradiation. For IB3 or locally advanced disease: Chemoradiation with cisplatin + etoposide ± brachytherapy, with consideration of neoadjuvant chemotherapy. Metastatic disease: Systemic therapy (platinum/etoposide ± immunotherapy) [55,56].
special_situations.evidence: Based on extrapolation from small cell lung cancer paradigms and retrospective cervical cancer series. No randomized trials specific to NECC [55].

special_situations.key_message: Fertility-sparing surgery is an option for select early-stage patients meeting strict criteria.
special_situations.management: For stage IA1 with no LVSI: Conization with negative margins. For IA1 with LVSI: Conization + SLN mapping/pelvic lymphadenectomy. For IA2–IB1 meeting all conservative criteria: Conization + SLN mapping/pelvic lymphadenectomy or radical trachelectomy. Criteria include: tumor ≤2 cm, depth ≤10 mm, negative margins, no LVSI (preferred), squamous or usual type adenocarcinoma grade 1-2 [5,6].
special_situations.evidence: Supported by prospective trials (ConCerv, GOG-0278, SHAPE) demonstrating low recurrence rates with conservative surgery in carefully selected patients [5,6,57].

clinical_pearls: The LACC trial established open abdominal radical hysterectomy as the standard over minimally invasive approaches for early-stage cervical cancer due to superior oncologic outcomes [58].

The SHAPE trial demonstrated that simple hysterectomy is non-inferior to radical hysterectomy for low-risk early-stage cervical cancer (tumor ≤2 cm, no LVSI) when combined with pelvic lymph node assessment [57].

SENTINEL lymph node mapping is now standard for surgical staging in early-stage disease, but adherence to the algorithm (side-specific dissection for failed mapping) and ultrastaging of SLNs are critical [19,59].
Concurrent cisplatin-based chemoradiation is the standard of care for stage IB3–IVA, with a 30–50% reduction in risk of death compared to radiation alone [8,60].
The addition of pembrolizumab to concurrent chemoradiation improves PFS and OS for patients with FIGO 2014 stage III–IVA cervical cancer (KEYNOTE-A18) [7].
PD-L1 testing (CPS ≥1) is required to guide first-line pembrolizumab-based therapy and second-line pembrolizumab monotherapy in recurrent/metastatic disease [27,61].

Comprehensive molecular profiling (including HER2, MSI/TMB, NTRK, RET) should be considered for all patients with recurrent or metastatic disease to identify actionable targets for second-line therapy [14,62].
Vaginal dilator use post-radiation is recommended to prevent vaginal stenosis and maintain sexual function [45].

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines in Oncology: Cervical Cancer, Version 2.2025
guidelines_resources.notes: This is the primary source document. Includes detailed algorithms, principles of surgery, radiation, systemic therapy, and survivorship [63].

guidelines_resources.title: SGO Clinical Practice Guideline: Follow-up for Gynecologic Cancer Survivors
guidelines_resources.notes: Provides specific recommendations for surveillance schedules and follow-up care post-treatment [42].

guidelines_resources.title: Radiation Therapy for Cervical Cancer: Executive Summary of an ASTRO Clinical Practice Guideline
guidelines_resources.notes: Focuses on RT techniques, planning, and dose constraints for definitive and adjuvant treatment [64].

guidelines_resources.organization: American Society for Colposcopy and Cervical Pathology (ASCCP)
guidelines_resources.title: Guidelines for Cervical Cancer Screening
guidelines_resources.notes: Referenced for primary cervical cancer screening (separate from management of invasive cancer) [65].

guidelines_resources.notes: Provides complementary perspective and recommendations on staging, treatment, and follow-up [66].

key_trials.full_name: Pembrolizumab or Placebo with Chemoradiotherapy Followed by Pembrolizumab or Placebo for Newly Diagnosed, High-Risk, Locally Advanced Cervical Cancer
key_trials.population: Previously untreated, high-risk locally advanced cervical cancer (FIGO 2014 stage IB2–IIB with node-positive disease or stage III–IVA)
key_trials.intervention: Pembrolizumab + concurrent platinum-based chemoradiation (CRT) followed by pembrolizumab maintenance
key_trials.comparator: Placebo + concurrent platinum-based CRT followed by placebo maintenance
key_trials.primary_endpoint: Progression-Free Survival (PFS) per RECIST v1.1 by investigator assessment
key_trials.key_result: Pembrolizumab + CRT significantly improved PFS vs placebo + CRT (HR 0.62, 95% CI 0.49–0.78; P<0.0001). Median PFS: 24.3 vs 16.1 months.
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Overall Survival (OS): At interim analysis, HR 0.73 (95% CI 0.56–0.95; P=0.013), favoring pembrolizumab. Overall Response Rate (ORR): 93% vs 85%.
key_trials.practice_change: Established pembrolizumab + concurrent CRT as a new standard of care for high-risk locally advanced cervical cancer, specifically for patients with FIGO 2014 stage IIIA–IIIB–IVA disease.

key_trials.full_name: A Phase 3, Randomized, Double-Blind Study of Pembrolizumab (MK-3475) in Combination with Chemotherapy With or Without Bevacizumab for the First-line Treatment of Persistent, Recurrent, or Metastatic Cervical Cancer
key_trials.population: Persistent, recurrent, or metastatic cervical cancer not previously treated with chemotherapy
key_trials.primary_endpoint: Overall Survival (OS) and Progression-Free Survival (PFS) in PD-L1 CPS ≥1 patients
key_trials.key_result: Pembrolizumab significantly improved OS vs placebo in patients with PD-L1 CPS ≥1 (median OS 28.6 vs 16.5 months; HR 0.60, 95% CI 0.49–0.74; P<0.0001). PFS also significantly improved (median 10.5 vs 8.2 months; HR 0.62, 95% CI 0.51–0.76; P<0.001).
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: ORR: 68.1% vs 50.2% in PD-L1 CPS ≥1 group. Duration of response (DoR): median 18.0 vs 10.4 months.
key_trials.practice_change: Established pembrolizumab + chemotherapy (± bevacizumab) as a preferred first-line standard for recurrent/metastatic cervical cancer with PD-L1 CPS ≥1.

key_trials.full_name: A Randomized Phase III Trial of Cisplatin-based Chemotherapy with or without Bevacizumab in Patients with Advanced, Recurrent, or Persistent Cervical Cancer
key_trials.population: Persistent, recurrent, or metastatic cervical cancer with no prior chemotherapy for advanced disease
key_trials.intervention: Chemotherapy (paclitaxel + cisplatin or paclitaxel + topotecan) + bevacizumab
key_trials.key_result: Addition of bevacizumab significantly improved OS (median 16.8 vs 13.3 months; HR 0.77, 95% CI 0.64–0.94; P=0.013). PFS was also improved (median 8.3 vs 6.2 months; HR 0.68, P<0.001).
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: ORR: 48% vs 36%. No significant difference in patient-reported quality of life.
key_trials.practice_change: Established bevacizumab as a key component of first-line therapy for recurrent/metastatic cervical cancer, leading to its approval and inclusion as a preferred regimen component.

key_trials.full_name: A Randomized, Open-Label, Phase 3 Trial of Atezolizumab Plus Bevacizumab and Chemotherapy for Metastatic, Persistent, or Recurrent Cervical Cancer
key_trials.population: Previously untreated metastatic, persistent, or recurrent cervical cancer not amenable to curative surgery/radiation
key_trials.key_result: Atezolizumab combination significantly improved PFS (median 13.7 vs 10.4 months; HR 0.62, 95% CI 0.49–0.78; P<0.0001). Interim OS also improved (median 32.1 vs 22.8 months; HR 0.68, 95% CI 0.52–0.88; P=0.0046).
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: ORR: 84% vs 72%. Duration of response longer with atezolizumab.
key_trials.practice_change: Established atezolizumab + bevacizumab + chemotherapy as another preferred first-line regimen for recurrent/metastatic cervical cancer, regardless of PD-L1 status in exploratory analysis.

key_trials.full_name: A Phase 3, Randomized, Open-Label Study of Tisotumab Vedotin Versus Investigator's Choice of Chemotherapy in Patients With Recurrent or Metastatic Cervical Cancer With Disease Progression On or After Chemoradiation
key_trials.population: Recurrent or metastatic cervical cancer with disease progression on or after chemotherapy (most had 1 prior line of systemic therapy in the metastatic setting)
key_trials.comparator: Investigator's choice chemotherapy (topotecan, vinorelbine, gemcitabine, irinotecan, or pemetrexed)
key_trials.key_result: Tisotumab vedotin significantly improved OS (median 11.5 vs 9.5 months; HR 0.70, 95% CI 0.54–0.89; P=0.004). PFS also improved (HR 0.67, P<0.0001). ORR: 17.8% vs 5.2%.
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Duration of response: median 10.3 vs 6.9 months. Safety profile consistent with known effects of TV.
key_trials.practice_change: Established tisotumab vedotin as a preferred second-line or later therapy for recurrent/metastatic cervical cancer after progression on chemotherapy.

key_trials.full_name: A Randomized, Open-Label, Phase 3 Study of Cemiplimab Versus Investigator's Choice Chemotherapy for the Treatment of Patients With Recurrent or Metastatic Cervical Cancer
key_trials.population: Recurrent or metastatic cervical cancer who progressed on or after platinum-based chemotherapy
key_trials.comparator: Investigator's choice chemotherapy (topotecan, vinorelbine, gemcitabine, irinotecan, pemetrexed)
key_trials.key_result: Cemiplimab significantly improved OS (median 12.0 vs 8.5 months; HR 0.69, 95% CI 0.56–0.84; P<0.001). Final analysis at 47.3 months follow-up confirmed OS benefit (HR 0.67). ORR: 16% vs 6.3%.
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: PFS: median 2.8 vs 2.9 months (HR 0.75). Benefit observed regardless of PD-L1 status, though greater magnitude in CPS ≥1 subgroup.
key_trials.practice_change: Established cemiplimab as an other recommended second-line or subsequent therapy for recurrent/metastatic cervical cancer.

key_trials.full_name: Nivolumab With or Without Ipilimumab in Patients With Recurrent or Metastatic Cervical Cancer
key_trials.population: Recurrent or metastatic cervical cancer (HPV-associated) with progression on or after chemotherapy
key_trials.intervention: Nivolumab + ipilimumab (NIVO1+IPI3 schedule: nivolumab 1 mg/kg + ipilimumab 3 mg/kg Q3W x4 doses, then nivolumab 240 mg Q2W)
key_trials.key_result: For the randomized NIVO1+IPI3 arm, ORR was 40% (95% CI 26–56). The pooled NIVO1+IPI3 schedule had ORR of 38%. Nivolumab monotherapy ORR was 26%.
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Duration of response not reached for many patients. Safety profile consistent with known irAEs for the combination.
key_trials.practice_change: Supported the addition of ipilimumab + nivolumab as an other recommended second-line or subsequent therapy option for recurrent/metastatic cervical cancer.

key_trials.full_name: Phase III Randomized Trial of Laparoscopic or Robotic Versus Abdominal Radical Hysterectomy in Patients With Early-Stage Cervical Cancer
key_trials.population: Patients with stage IA1 (with LVSI) to IB1 cervical cancer
key_trials.intervention: Minimally invasive radical hysterectomy (MIS: laparoscopic or robotic)
key_trials.primary_endpoint: Rate of disease-free survival (DFS) at 3 years
key_trials.key_result: MIS was associated with lower 3-year DFS (91.2% vs 97.1%; HR 3.74, 95% CI 1.63–8.58) and lower 3-year OS (93.8% vs 99.0%; HR 6.00, 95% CI 1.77–20.30).
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Higher rate of adjuvant therapy in MIS group. No difference in perioperative complications.
key_trials.practice_change: Changed surgical practice to favor open abdominal radical hysterectomy for early-stage cervical cancer, as MIS was found to be inferior for oncologic outcomes.

key_trials.full_name: Simple Versus Radical Hysterectomy in Women With Low-Risk Cervical Cancer
key_trials.population: Low-risk cervical cancer (tumor ≤2 cm, limited stromal invasion, node-negative)
key_trials.intervention: Simple (extrafascial) hysterectomy with pelvic lymph node assessment
key_trials.comparator: Radical hysterectomy (type B/C) with pelvic lymph node assessment
key_trials.key_result: Non-inferiority of simple hysterectomy was demonstrated. 3-year pelvic recurrence rate: 2.52% (simple) vs 2.17% (radical). The difference was within the pre-specified non-inferiority margin.
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Significantly lower rate of urinary retention with simple hysterectomy (0.6% vs 11% at 4 weeks, P<0.001). Similar rates of adjuvant therapy.
key_trials.practice_change: Supports the use of simple hysterectomy with lymph node assessment as a standard treatment option for low-risk early-stage cervical cancer (tumor ≤2 cm, no LVSI, negative margins), offering less morbidity.
